chapter: System of Particles and Rotational Motion
locked_facts: Torque τ = r × F = rF sinθ (cross product)
Torque is maximum when force is perpendicular to position vector (θ = 90°)
Torque is zero when force passes through the axis of rotation
A couple consists of two equal and opposite forces with different lines of action
Moment of a couple = F × d (force × perpendicular distance between forces)
A couple produces pure rotation with no translation
The moment of a couple is the same about any point — it is a free vector
τ = Iα (Newton's second law for rotation)
truth_statements: Torque is the rotational analogue of force — it causes angular acceleration
The magnitude of torque depends on the force, the distance from the axis, and the angle between them
A couple produces rotation without any net translational force on the body
The torque of a couple is independent of the point about which it is calculated
For equilibrium of a rigid body both the net force and net torque must be zero

aha_moment.what_to_show: A wrench with force applied at different angles. At θ = 90° (perpendicular), torque is maximum and the nut turns easily. At θ = 0° (along the handle), torque is zero — no rotation at all. The sinθ factor makes the connection visceral.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Force arrow rotating from 0° to 90° with torque bar growing from zero to maximum
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students think any force causes rotation — seeing zero torque when force passes through the axis makes the r × F formula click

simulation_states.label: Torque defined
simulation_states.description: A door with force applied at the handle. τ = rF sinθ shown. The perpendicular distance (moment arm) is highlighted.
simulation_states.emphasis: Torque = force × moment arm. Longer arm = easier rotation.

simulation_states.label: Angle matters
simulation_states.description: Same force applied at different angles to a wrench. At 90°: maximum torque. At 0°: zero torque. At 45°: intermediate. Torque bar updates.
simulation_states.emphasis: Only the perpendicular component of force contributes to torque

simulation_states.label: Couple
simulation_states.description: Two equal opposite forces on a steering wheel. No net force (no translation) but net torque (pure rotation). Moment of couple = F × d, shown to be same about any point.
simulation_states.emphasis: A couple produces pure rotation — the moment is the same about ANY point

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts force magnitude, angle, and point of application on a rigid body. Torque value and rotation animation update in real time. Toggle couple mode with two forces.
simulation_states.emphasis: Change angle and distance — find the combination that maximises torque

common_misconceptions.belief: Any force applied to a body causes rotation
common_misconceptions.correction: A force causes rotation only if it has a non-zero torque about the axis — i.e., it doesn't pass through the axis. A force through the pivot produces zero torque and zero rotation.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Apply force through the pivot point — body doesn't rotate despite the force

common_misconceptions.correction: A couple consists of two equal and opposite forces, so net force is zero — no translation is possible. A couple causes ONLY rotation.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show net force vector = 0 while body rotates under a couple
